coredata.dc:title: Enhanced full solar spectrum photocatalysis by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated BiO2-x nanosheets: Ultrafast charge transfer and molecular oxygen activation
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Fei
Liu, Lian-Lian
Zhang, Ying-Jie
Wu, Jing-Hang
Huang, Gui-Xiang
Yang, Qi
Chen, Jie-Jie
Yu, Han-Qing
coredata.dc:description: It is still challenging to produce superoxide and hydroxyl radicals through activating molecular oxygen under broad-spectrum light in environmental photocatalysis. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) modified BiO2-x nanosheets were successfully fabricated and exhibited superior performance in light-harvesting, electron-hole pair separation, and full-spectrum driven molecular oxygen activation. The hybridized photocatalyst with a N-GQDs weight ratio of 0.4wt% (GBO-0.4) exhibited an excellent photocatalytic activity toward tetracycline degradation with a 4.0-fold, 2.9-fold and 5.5-fold higher reaction rate under full-spectrum, visible and near-infrared light irradiations than that of pure BiO2-x, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the electron collection effect and up-conversion photoluminescence properties of the N-GQDs as well as the synergistic effects of the developed nanojunction. Efficient molecular oxygen activation was achieved via the construction of a bulk-to-surface channel between BiO2-x and N-GQDs. DFT calculations were also used to explore the geometric and electronic structure variations of BiO2-x after the introduction of N-GQDs. The high photostability and mineralization ability toward tetracycline degradation confirm the promising application prospects of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x photocatalyst. This work provides a rational strategy for designing 0D/2D nanoscale heterostructure photocatalysts with improved full-spectrum photoactivity for environmental applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Full spectrum
Molecular oxygen activation
N-GQDs/BiO2-x
Photocatalytic degradation mechanism
Tetracycline

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Active species trapping and ESR measurements
Band structures, photoelectrical and charge carrier separation properties
TC degradation pathway by the GBO-0.4 composite under full-spectrum light

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC0406303), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21590812, 51538011, 51908528 and 51821006), the Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Support Program of China (BX20180290), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2018M640595) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WK2060120001) for supporting this work. The numerical calculations in this work have been conducted on the supercomputing system in the Supercomputing Center of University of Science and Technology of China.

The authors thank the National Key R&D Program of China ( 2018YFC0406303 ), the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21590812 , 51538011 , 51908528 and 51821006 ), the Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Support Program of China ( BX20180290 ), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2018M640595 ) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities ( WK2060120001 ) for supporting this work. The numerical calculations in this work have been conducted on the supercomputing system in the Supercomputing Center of University of Science and Technology of China .

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the proposed photocatalytic mechanism over the GBO-0.4 composite towards TC degradation under full-spectrum light irradiation.
Proposed TC transformation pathways on the GBO-0.4 catalyst under full-spectrum light irradiation.
TEM images of (a) BiO2-x and (b, c) GBO-0.4; (d-g) represent the corresponding EDS mapping images of Bi, O, C and N elements.
XPS patterns of the GBO-0.4 sample: (a) survey, (b) Bi 4f, (c) O 1s and (d) C 1s; UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectra (e) and photoluminescence spectra (f) of the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4.
Transient photocurrent responses and EIS Nyquist plots of the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4 under (a, b) full-spectrum light, (c, d) visible light and (e, f) near-infrared light irradiations.

Photocatalytic degradation of TC by different photocatalysts under (a) full-spectrum light, (c) visible light and (e) near-infrared light irradiations and (b, d, f) represent the correspondent pseudo-first-order kinetic constants.
Trapping experiments for the photocatalytic degradation of TC with different scavengers under (a, b) full-spectrum light, (c, d) visible light and (e, f) near-infrared light irradiations.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for DMPO-
O2 − (methanol dispersion) under (a, b) full-spectrum light, (c, d) visible light and (e, f) near-infrared light irradiations.

DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for DMPO-
OH (aqueous dispersion) under (a, b) full-spectrum light, (c, d) visible light and (e, f) near-infrared light irradiations.

(a) ESR signal of oxygen vacancy of the GBO-0.4, (b) TC removal efficiency under full-spectrum light by the GBO-0.4 treated with H2O2 for different times and (c) TC degradation results with the GBO-0.4 firstly oxidized with H2O2 for 2 h and followed by 1 h treatment of NaBH4.

Electron density difference diagram (a) of N-GQDs on the BiO2-x (110) surface, where green represents the electron-rich region and yellow represents the electron-depleted region. Total and partial density of states (b) for N-GQDs/BiO2-x. Work functions of BiO2-x (110) surface (c) and N-GQDs/BiO2-x (d) (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article).

head.ce:title: Enhanced full solar spectrum photocatalysis by nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots decorated BiO2-x nanosheets: Ultrafast charge transfer and molecular oxygen activation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Department of Applied Chemistry, University of Science & Technology of China, Hefei, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion
University of Science & Technology of China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Environmental Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University), Ministry of Education, Changsha, 410082, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Environmental Biology and Pollution Control (Hunan University), Ministry of Education, Changsha 410082, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: It is still challenging to produce superoxide and hydroxyl radicals through activating molecular oxygen under broad-spectrum light in environmental photocatalysis. In this work, nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) modified BiO2-x nanosheets were successfully fabricated and exhibited superior performance in light-harvesting, electron-hole pair separation, and full-spectrum driven molecular oxygen activation. The hybridized photocatalyst with a N-GQDs weight ratio of 0.4wt% (GBO-0.4) exhibited an excellent photocatalytic activity toward tetracycline degradation with a 4.0-fold, 2.9-fold and 5.5-fold higher reaction rate under full-spectrum, visible and near-infrared light irradiations than that of pure BiO2-x, respectively. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was ascribed to the electron collection effect and up-conversion photoluminescence properties of the N-GQDs as well as the synergistic effects of the developed nanojunction. Efficient molecular oxygen activation was achieved via the construction of a bulk-to-surface channel between BiO2-x and N-GQDs. DFT calculations were also used to explore the geometric and electronic structure variations of BiO2-x after the introduction of N-GQDs. The high photostability and mineralization ability toward tetracycline degradation confirm the promising application prospects of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x photocatalyst. This work provides a rational strategy for designing 0D/2D nanoscale heterostructure photocatalysts with improved full-spectrum photoactivity for environmental applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Semiconductor photocatalysis is a promising avenue to address the increasing environmental pollution and energy crisis issues [1,2]. However, conventional semiconductor photocatalysts such as TiO2 [3,4], ZnO [5,6], CdS [7] are largely limited by the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, narrow solar light absorption and low quantum yield and optical instability (for ZnO and CdS). Recently, bismuth (Bi)-based photocatalysts, e.g., BiVO4 [8–10], BiOX (X = Cl [11–13], Br [14,15], I [16–18]), Bi2MoO6 [19], Bi2SiO5 [20], bismuth oxide [21,22], have emerged as an attractive alternative attributable to their simple synthesis, wide spectrum light absorption, high catalytic activity, non-toxicity and structural stability. The light absorption of such bismuth oxides can be broadened by adjusting the molar ratio of Bi and O. For example, the threshold of light absorption for commercial Bi2O3 was estimated to be 460 nm [23], while the photo-absorption of Bi2O4 nanorods was extended to about 620 nm [24]. Unfortunately, most of them fail to absorb near-infrared light energy, which accounts for ∼49% in the solar spectrum [25]. To take full advantage of the whole solar energy, BiO2-x nanosheets were fabricated based on VBi-O’’’ vacancy engineering and exhibited a UV–vis-NIR responsive photocatalytic activity [26]. However, the rapid electron/hole pair recombination, poor charge mobility and the underutilization of reactive species are still stumbling blocks.

The above problem of a single BiO2-x system might be resolved by constructing effective surface separation centers to prolong the charge carrier lifetime, increasing reactive sites and accelerating interface redox reactions. In this respect, ultrasmall nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) might be a promising candidate to be integrated with the BiO2-x matrix. Compared with the large-size materials that possess many surface defects to serve as the electron-hole recombination centers at the interfaces, N-GQDs as a zero-dimension (0D) carbon material can reduce the number of interfacial defects. N-GQDs have been widely studied in the fields of electrochemical biosensor, photovoltaic device, and energy conversion due to their low cost, limited toxicity, high chemical stability, excellent electric conductivity, unique edge and quantum confinement effects [10,27]. In particular, the up-converted photoluminescence properties make the N-GQDs a good photosensitizer to capture visible and NIR light [28]. By taking advantage of their electron transfer/reservoir abilities, N-GQDs can also be used for developing hybrid photocatalysts to accelerate the interfacial charge carrier migration, prolong the charge lifetime and reduce the light-blocking effect [29]. Several N-GQDs-based 0D/2D systems such as N-GQDs-BiOI/MnNb2O6 [30], N-GQDs-Bi2WO6/g-C3N4 [31], Ag/N-GQDs/g-C3N4 [32] have been developed. These 0D/2D hybrid materials largely suppress the recombination of electron/hole pairs due to their abundant electron separation centers, although the relationship between the individual materials and interface structure-performance remains unclear.

Inspired by the aforementioned analyses, we hypothesize that more efficient photocatalysts that can utilize the whole solar spectrum might be constructed by dosing N-GQDs onto BiO2-x nanosheets to form nanoscale heterojunctions. Such a hybrid photocatalyst may have several advantages. First, the intimate interface contact between the N-GQDs and BiO2-x favors the formation of more N-GQDs and BiO2-x planar interface. Secondly, a bulk-to-surface channel for electron transfer from BiO2-x to N-GQDs can be constructed, which favors fast interfacial electron transport through the electron tunneling mechanism. Thirdly, there will be synergistic effects between individual materials (including light absorption, charge separation, and reactive oxygen species generation), resulting in more efficient wider-ranged light absorption attributable to the up-converted photoluminescence effect of the N-GQDs and the strengthened redox ability of the generated active species. Finally, the charge carrier recombination from the interior to the surface or at the surface of BiO2-x will be effectively restrained by introducing the N-GQDs. As a result, more photoexcited charges will be utilized to activate molecular oxygen, resulting in the formation of more reactive oxygen species (ROS, such as
O2 −, H2O2, and the hydroxyl radical:
OH) to accelerate pollutant degradation. However, the activation of molecular oxygen (O2) into
O2 − via a one-electron reduction under broad-spectrum light, especially in the near-infrared region, is still of great challenge. Our design of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite in this work would be beneficial to efficiently address these issues.

To achieve the above goal, a simple hydrothermal method was used to prepare BiO2-x nanosheets and the N-GQDs/BiO2-x hybrid materials were constructed by in-situ depositing N-GQDs onto BiO2-x nanosheets. The photocatalytic activity of the hybrid materials for pollutant degradation was evaluated by using a colorless compound tetracycline (TC) as a model pollutant under full-spectrum, visible and NIR light irradiations. The light-harvesting capacity and surface electron/hole separation efficiency were analyzed using the UV–vis-NIR diffuse reflectance spectrum (UV–vis-NIR DRS) and photoelectrochemical tests. Finally, a tentative mechanism for the enhanced photocatalytic performance in the broad-spectral region was proposed.

Sodium bismuthate (NaBiO3), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), triammonium citrate (C6H5O7(NH4)3) tetracycline hydrochloride (TC), sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4), tert-butyl alcohol (TBA), benzoquinone (BQ), methanol, ethanol, and nitric acid were all purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., China or Shanghai Chemical Reagents Co., China. All reagents were of analytical grade and used as received without further treatment. All solutions were prepared with Milli-Q water with a resistivity of 18.25 MΩ/cm.

The N-GQDs were synthesized by using a facile reflux method under the atmospheric pressure. Briefly, 2.0 g of C6H5O7(NH4)3 was first dissolved into 40 mL of H2O and then transferred into a round-bottom flask equipped with a condenser tube. The flask was heated to 200 °C for 30 min in an oil bath. The pH was adjusted to 7.0 by dosing 10 mg/mL NaOH solution. Finally, the solid N-GQDs were obtained by a freeze-drying process.

In a typical synthesis process of BiO2-x nanosheets, 2.4 g NaOH was dosed into 50 mL of deionized water under magnetic stirring, followed by adding 2.8 g NaBiO3. The mixture was stirred for an extra 60 min at room temperature, then transferred into a 100 mL Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 18 h. The precipitates were collected, washed with deionized water and ethanol for three times, and dried at 80 °C for 12 h in a vacuum oven.

The N-GQDs/BiO2-x hybrid materials were prepared as follows: first, 5 mL N-GQDs (1 mg/mL) solution was prepared in advance. The BiO2-x nanosheets (0.1 g) were dispersed into 60 mL deionized water and sonicated for 1 h to obtain a homogeneous suspension. Then, a certain volume of N-GQDs solution was dosed into the BiO2-x suspension and stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the products were obtained by filtrating, washing and drying at 80 °C for 12 h. For clarification, the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composites with the expected N-GQDs volume of 0.2, 0.4 and 0.6 mL were denoted as GBO-0.2, GBO-0.4, and GBO-0.6, respectively.

The X-ray powder diffraction patterns of the catalysts were recorded with a Philips X’ Pert PRO SUPER X-ray diffractometer (XRD) with Cu Kα X-ray irradiation at 40 kV and 40 mA. A field emission scanning electron microscope (FESEM, S-4800, Hitachi Co., Japan) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) with a FEI Tecnai G20 (Hitachi Co., Japan) were used to analyze the surface morphology. UV–vis-NIR DRS of the prepared samples was measured by a spectrometer (UV-3700, Shimadzu Co., Japan) in a range of 200–2400 nm. The valence states of the constituent elements were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an ESCALAB 250 spectrometer (Thermo Fisher Inc., USA). The surface area was measured using a Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) method with a Builder 4200 instrument (Tristar II 3020 M, Micromeritics Co., USA). The photoluminescence (PL) spectra were characterized by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Aqualog, Horiba Co., Japan).

The photocatalytic activities of the as-synthesized samples were estimated by the degradation of TC at 25 ºC with a circulating water system. A 300 W Xe arc lamp (PLS/SXE 300D/300DUV, Beijing Perfect light Co., China) was used the light source. The Xe lamp was equipped with no filter as the full-spectrum light source, a 420 nm cutoff filter as the visible light source and a 700 nm cutoff filter as the NIR light source. Before light irradiation, 0.04 g of the prepared photocatalyst was completely dispersed into a 60-mL aqueous solution containing 10 mg/L TC and the suspension was stirred for an additional 30 min in the dark to ensure sufficient adsorption-desorption equilibrium between the pollutant and catalyst. A quantitative of the reaction solution was withdrawn at given time intervals under light irradiation and then centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 10 min to remove the photocatalyst. The residual TC concentration was measured using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2450, Shimadzu Co., Japan) at its maximum absorption peak of 357 nm. The TC degradation products were identified by a liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (LC–MS) system equipped with a 6460 HPLC (Agilent Inc, USA, with a Kromasil C18 column (4.6 × 250 mm)) and an API 3000 mass analyzer. A mixture of 60% acetonitrile and 40% deionized water (containing 0.1 % formic acid) was used as the mobile phase at a flow rate of 1.0 mL·min−1. The total organic carbon (TOC) measurement was performed on a TOC analyzer (MutiN/C-2100, Analytik Jena AG, Germany).

The photoelectrochemical characteristics of the prepared catalysts were measured by an electrochemical workstation (CHI760D, CH Instrument Co., China) equipped with a standard three-electrode system. A saturated Ag/AgCl electrode was used as the reference electrode, a platinum wire was employed as the counter electrode. The photocatalyst coated onto a F-doped SnO2 (FTO) conductive glass served as the working electrode and prepared. Briefly, the FTO substrates were orderly cleaned with acetone, ethanol, deionized water and ethanol for the corresponding 1 h. Then, the obtained FTO glasses were dried at 60 °C for 30 min in an oven. Subsequently, 5 mg powder photocatalyst was dispersed into a mixed solution containing ethanol (0.2 mL) + deionized water (0.1 mL) + IPA (0.1 mL) under ultrasonication treatment for 1 h. Later, 10 μL Nafion solution was dosed and constantly sonicated for another 30 min. Finally, the formed slurries (0.2 mL) were dropped onto a 1 cm×4 cm FTO with an effective working area of 2 cm2 and dried at 200 °C for 2 h. The Na2SO4 (0.2 M) aqueous solution served as the electrolyte. A 300 W Xe lamp (PLS/SXE 300D/300DUV, Beijing Perfect light Co., China) with different light cutoff filters was used as the light source. The photocurrent was obtained by the i-t curve measurement with a bias voltage of 0 V. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis was recorded by applying an alternating current voltage of 5 mV amplitude in the frequency range from 105 to 1 Hz. Mott-Schottky plots were obtained at a frequency of 1 kHz and an applied voltage range of -1.5-0 V.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Active species trapping and ESR measurements
ce:section.ce:para:
To detect the active species generated in the photocatalytic reaction, different scavengers at a concentration of 10 mM were dosed into the TC solution before light reaction. Benzoquinone (BQ), isopropanol (IPA) and sodium oxalate (Na2C2O4) were respectively selected as the scavengers of superoxide radical (
O2 −), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and holes (h+). The experiments under K2Cr2O7 (10 mM) addition and N2 saturated conditions were conducted to examine the role of electrons and the source of O2 respectively. Furthermore, electron spin resonance (ESR) signal spin-trapped by reagent 5, 5-dimethyl-l-pyrroline N-oxide (DMPO) with an ESR spectrometer (ER200-SRC, Bruker Co., USA) was used to reconfirm the formation of
O2 − and
OH radicals. Each sample (2 mg) was first dissolved into the 0.5 mL methanol (DMPO-
O2 −) or 0.5 mL deionized water (
OH). Then, 10 μL DMPO was dosed into the ultrasonic dispersion for 2 min in the dark. Finally, ESR signals under both dark and light conditions were obtained.

DFT calculations were carried out using the plane-wave pseudopotential in the Cambridge Sequential Total Energy Package (CASTEP). The [110] oriented BiO2 layers were presented by periodically repeating along the [110] direction of the unit cell. The BiO2-x (110) models were constructed with a series of oxygen vacancies (Ov) concentrations. The thickness of the vacuum layer is 20 Å. The N-GQDs model in N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite structure was fabricated based on the graphene layer and contained 12C atoms, 3O atoms, and 2N atoms. The dangling bonds N-GQD was saturated with hydrogen atoms. Generalized gradient approximation (GGA) within the Perdew-Burke-Ernzerh (PBE) of exchange-correlation functional was applied. The total energy converge was set to 1×10−5 eV per atom and the convergence criterion of the force between atoms was 0.03 eV/Å. The maximum displacement was 1×10−3 Å and the maximum stress was 0.05 GPa. An energy cutoff of 571.4 eV and the k-point sets of 4×4×4 were used for optimizing the BiO2 bulk phase. Additionally, an energy cutoff of 340 eV and the k-point sets of 1×2×1 were used for the calculations of BiO2-x (110) surface and N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite structure.

Fig. S1 depicts the XRD patterns of the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4 samples. All the diffraction peaks of the BiO2-x could be readily indexed to the cubic phase of the BiO2-x (JCPDS No. 47-1057 [33]). The XRD patterns of the GBO-0.4 matched well with BiO2-x and no characteristic peaks of the N-GQDs were found due to its high dispersion and low content in the composite [27]. No miscellaneous peaks of other phases were detected. All the characteristic peaks were strong and sharp, indicating a high purity and crystallinity of the prepared samples.

The TEM images show that the BiO2-x was in the form of a typical nanosheet and its morphology was not influenced by the introduction of N-GQDs (Fig. 1 a and b). In the magnified TEM image of GBO-0.4 (Fig. 1c), the marked black plots could be assigned to the N-GQDs. The combination and spatial distribution of the GBO-0.4 sample were further revealed by TEM-based energy-disperse X-ray spectroscopy images (TEM-EDS mapping). As shown in Fig. 1d–h, the Bi, O, C and N elements coexisted and were homogeneously distributed, indicating that N-GQDs were successfully introduced onto the BiO2-x nanosheets.

The surface composition and chemical state of the GBO-0.4 sample were analyzed by XPS. The survey XPS spectrum reveals that the co-existence of chemical elements Bi, O, C and N on the surface of GBO-0.4 (Fig. 2 a). In the Bi 4f high resolution spectrum (Fig. 2b), two typical peaks at 163.68 eV and 158.41 eV could be assigned to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively [19,34]. Two XPS signals of O 1s (Fig. 2c) for the GBO-0.4 were located at 531.51 eV and 529.24 eV, corresponding to the Ov and lattice oxygen (Bi-O), respectively [33,35]. These results verify an oxygen-rich-state of the GBO-0.4 composite. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 2d) was convoluted into four peaks, which were respectively attributed to the CO bond (288.88 eV), CO bond (285.85 eV), CN bond (284.86 eV) and CC/CC bonds (284.57 eV), respectively [10,36]. These results demonstrate that the N-GQDs/BiO2-x hybrid was successfully prepared as designed.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Band structures, photoelectrical and charge carrier separation properties
ce:section.ce:para: UV–vis-NIR DRS were recorded to explore the optical absorption properties and energy band features of the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4 samples. The light absorption edge of the pure BiO2-x was determined at approximately 852 nm (Fig. 2e). After the introduction of N-GQDs, its light-harvesting capacity was strengthened and the absorption edge was extended to 895 nm, indicating a good upconversion ability of the N-GQDs. From the empirical equation (Eg = 1240/λ, where λ is the absorption edge and Eg is the band gap energy), the band gap energies for the pure BiO2-x and GBO-0.4 were estimated to be 1.46 and 1.39 eV, respectively. Thus, the GBO-0.4 composite could effectively harness the wider spectral (including NIR light region) of solar light.

To gain an in-depth insight into the interior charge transfer and separation efficiency of the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4, the PL spectra, transient photocurrent responses, EIS Nyquist plots were examined. PL spectra provide corroborative evidence to evaluate the transfer and separation efficiency of the photoexcited charge for photocatalysts. Generally, a lower PL intensity suggests a higher separation ability of charge carriers [37]. The GBO-0.4 showed a much lower PL emission peak intensity than the pure BiO2-x, at room temperature (Fig. 2f), indicating a decreased recombination of the photo-generated electrons and holes, which favored photogenerated charges in the GBO-0.4. Thus, the introduction of N-GQDs was conducive to the separation of the photogenerated electron/hole pairs. The photocurrent of the different materials in four cycles of intermittent on-off irradiation was monitored. A higher current density was achieved by the GBO-0.4 than the pristine BiO2-x. The charge carrier density of the GBO-0.4 was approximately 5.1, 6.0 and 8.8 times higher than that of the BiO2-x under full-spectrum (Fig. 3 a), visible (Fig. 3c) and NIR light (Fig. 3e), respectively. For the GBO-0.4, the photogenerated electrons could be rapidly transferred from the conduction band (CB) of BiO2-x to N-GQDs, thereby reducing the recombination of electron-hole pairs. More reactive oxygen species were produced by the GBO-0.4 under light irradiation, which favored pollutant degradation. The charge transfer resistance of the photocatalysts was estimated based on the EIS Nyquist plots. As shown in Fig. 3b, d, f, the GBO-0.4 displayed a better electronic conductivity and smaller arc diameter under both full-spectrum, visible and even NIR light irradiations, indicating a lower interfacial charge transfer resistance than the BiO2-x nanosheet.

All these results demonstrate that the introduction of N-GQDs enhanced light-harvesting and accelerated the charge separation and transfer from the bulk to the interface of BiO2-x nanosheet, which might favor more efficient pollutant degradation in the entire spectrum region.

The adsorption-desorption equilibrium was obtained within 10 min and the enhanced adsorption capacity was observed after the introduction of N-GQDs (Fig. S2). The photocatalytic TC degradation performances by the GBO-0.4 under full-spectrum light (λ>320 nm), visible light (λ>420 nm) and NIR light (λ>700 nm) were evaluated. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the GBO-0.4 achieved 97.8% degradation of TC within 60 min, which far outperformed the BiO2-x (62.9%). The pseudo-first-order equation ((-ln(Ct /C0 )=kt, where k is the reaction rate constant, Ct is the TC concentration at the reaction time of t and C0 is the initial TC concentration) was applied to describe the kinetic behavior. The degradation rate constant k value of the GBO-0.4 under full-spectrum irradiation (0.0621 min−1) was 4.0 times higher than that of the BiO2-x (Fig. 4b). Consistent results were obtained under visible and NIR light irradiations (Fig. 4c and e). The GBO-0.4 exhibited 2.9-fold and 5.5-fold higher degradation activity than the BiO2-x, respectively (Fig. 4d and f). The long-wavelength light could be absorbed and converted into the short-wavelength light with the co-existence of the N-GQDs. As a result, more electron/hole pairs would be generated and be involved in TC degradation.

The radical scavenging experiments under different light irradiation conditions were carried out to explore the formed reactive species in the photocatalytic degradation process. The holes (h+), hydroxyl radical (
OH) and superoxide radical (
O2 −) were determined by using Na2C2O4, IPA and BQ as scavengers respectively [4,25]. As shown in Fig. 5 , all the scavengers suppressed the TC degradation in the BiO2-x/full light system. The TC degradation efficiency decreased by 58.3%, 49.4% and 11.6% in the presence of Na2C2O4, BQ and IPA, respectively. Thus, h+,
O2 − and
OH contributed to TC degradation by the order: h+>
O2 − >
OH (Fig. 5a). For the GBO-0.4, a result similar to BiO2-x was observed under full-spectrum light irradiation (Fig. 5b). The addition of Na2C2O4, IPA or BQ also substantially decreased the TC removal efficiency from 97.8% to 3.1%, 8.5%, and 79.3%, respectively. Again, similar results were also obtained under visible and NIR light irradiations (Fig. 5c–f), where h+,
O2 – and
OH contributed to the TC degradation. Especially, the role of
O2 − was distinctly promoted under NIR light irradiation. The TC degradation by the GBO-0.4 was obviously depressed after dosing BQ under NIR irradiation, indicating that
O2 − was still an important contributor. In contrast, for the pure BiO2-x,
O2 − radicals had a smaller contribution to the TC degradation under NIR light. These results demonstrate that the introduction of N-GQDs enhanced the redox ability of the photogenerated holes and electrons.

The formation of ROS such as
O2 − and
OH was validated by the ESR spin-trapping spectra with DMPO as the trapping agent. As displayed in Fig. 6 , no signal was detected in the dark for the BiO2-x and GBO-0.4. However, four characteristic peaks ascribed to DMPO-
O2 − adducts were observed under light irradiation and the peak intensity increased with the irradiation time. The
O2 − signal produced in the GBO-0.4 photocatalytic system after 5-min irradiation was about 1.6-fold (full-wavelength light, Fig. 6a, and b), 1.5-fold (visible light, Fig. 6c, and d) and 2.4-fold (NIR light, Fig. 6e, and f) higher than that of the pure BiO2-x, respectively. The introduction of N-GQDs onto BiO2-x nanosheets facilitated the photogenerated electron/hole pair separation and more electrons became available for the activation of molecular oxygen via single-electron reduction, especially in the NIR region. Similarly, a four-line ESR signal corresponding to the DMPO-
OH adducts was also observed under light irradiation (Fig. 7 ). The
OH radicals were generated over both BiO2-x and GBO-0.4, which coincided well with the active species trapping results. The pure BiO2-x showed much weaker
OH radicals generation than the GBO-0.4 under full-spectrum (Fig. 7a and b), visible (Fig. 7c and d) and NIR light (Fig. 7e and f) irradiations. Notably, the GBO-0.4 showed nearly 5.7 times higher
OH radicals than pure BiO2-x. The up-converted photoluminescence and electron reservoir properties of the N-GQDs could substantially improve full-spectrum light utilization and enhance molecular oxygen activation. As a result, more reactive oxygen species such as
OH and
O2 – were produced under the broader spectrum light, which was beneficial to the TC degradation.

Furthermore, the role of Ov in the GBO-0.4 sample was also evaluated. As shown in Fig. 8 a, an obvious signal at a g-value of 2.0 was found, which was identified as the electrons trapped by Ov. This result is in line with O 1s XPS analysis. The existence of Ov also played a role in TC degradation. Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) was used to oxidize the GBO-0.4 sample and eliminate the Ov on its surface (Fig. 8b). After 0.5-h H2O2 treatment, the photocatalytic performance under full-spectrum irradiation declined. After being treated with H2O2 for 1.0 h, the GBO-0.4 showed a decreased TC degradation efficiency from 97.8% to 62.2%. However, further increasing the H2O2 treatment time caused no changes in the TC removal, likely attributable to the disappearance of Ov. To reconfirm this, the sample was treated with NaBH4 to tentatively recover the Ov through the reduction process. After 2.0-h treatment of the oxidized GBO-0.4 sample with 0.1 M NaBH4, the TC removal was recovered to 95.9% (Fig. 8c), which was of a similar level to that of the initial GBO-0.4 sample. These results indicate that Ov did play a crucial role in TC degradation.

Mott-Schottky measurements were used to estimate the flat band potential of BiO2-x (Eq. (1)) [38]:
(1)
1
C 2
=
2
q ε
ε 0
N d
E -
E
f b
-
K b
T
q
where C is the space charge capacitance, Nd is the density of charge carriers, q is the electronic charge (1.602 × 10−19 C), ε and ε0 represent the dielectric constant of the semiconductor and permittivity in a vacuum, respectively. E and Efb refer to the applied potential and flat-band potential, respectively. kB is the Boltzmann constant, T is the temperature in Kelvin.

The BiO2-x could be regarded as an n-type semiconductor due to the positive slope in the Mott-Schottky curve (Fig. S9). The Efb value of the BiO2-x was calculated to be -0.80 V vs. Ag/AgCl electrode. The Efb value could be further converted into a normal hydrogen electrode (NHE) potential using the following equation (Eq. (2)) [39].
(2)
E
f b ( v s .
N H E )
=
E
f b ( p H = 0
v s .
A g / A g C l )
+
E
A g C l
+ 0.059
p H
where the pH of the electrolyte is 6.8 and EAgCl is 0.197 V. Consequently, the Efb of BiO2-x was -0.20 V vs. NHE. The conduction band minimum (CBM) of a typical n-type semiconductor is 0.3 V below Efb , the CBM values of BiO2-x was thus determined to be -0.50 eV vs. NHE. With the relationship (Eg =EVB -ECB, EVB, and ECB are the valence band position and conduction band position of the semiconductor, respectively), the EVB was calculated to be +0.96 eV.

Compared with the standard reduction potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV), the BiO2-x exhibited a more negative CB position (-0.50 eV). Thus, the photogenerated electrons could capture the dissolved oxygen to produce
O2 −. To reconfirm this observation, nitroblue tetrazolium (NBT) transformation of the pure BiO2-x and GBO-0.4 sample were conducted under full-spectrum light irradiation. As shown in Fig. S10a and S10b, the UV–vis spectra of NBT declined with the increasing irradiation time, suggesting that photo-induced electrons could rapidly react with O2 to generate
O2 − via one-electron reduction. The peak intensity at 259 nm in the NBT spectrum of the GBO-0.4 sample declined more prominently than the BiO2-x. Thus, more
O2 − radicals were produced after the introduction of N-GQDs and molecular oxygen activation was remarkably strengthened. This result is consistent with the radical trapping and ESR results.

To explore the electronic structure variations of BiO2-x after the introduction of N-GQDs, the density of states and the electron density difference were investigated by DFT calculations. The band gap of BiO2-x (110) was significantly reduced with an increase in Ov concentration (Fig. S11), indicating that the light absorption of BiO2-x was enhanced. Moreover, the calculated band gap value of BiO1.75 (110) was 1.34 eV (Fig. S11 h), which is close to the experimental value (1.46 eV), implying that the Bi/O atomic ratio of the as-synthesized BiO2-x might approach 1:1.75. For the BiO2-x with less Ov content (BiO1.83 (110), Fig. S11f), the CB was mainly composed of 4p-orbitals of the Bi atoms, while the VB was mainly constructed of 2p-orbitals of the O atoms. As the Ov concentration increased (BiO1.75 (110), Fig. S11i and BiO1.67 (110), Fig. S11 l), the localized electronic states composed of Bi 4p-orbitials appeared below the Fermi level, constituting the defect energy level [13,14,39]. Bi atoms around the Ov became a donor to generate the photo-generated electrons under visible light. To effectively promote charge transfer from Ov of BiO2-x (110) to the substrate, N-GQDs (Fig. S12a, b) were introduced to form a 0D/2D heterojunction. The shortest distance among the neighboring hydrogen-terminated N-GQDs in the supercells was 6.61 Å (Fig. S13), which is longer than 5 Å. Thus, the interaction between N-GQDs in the adjacent supercells could be ignored. The electron density difference was performed to analyze the charge transfer between N-GQDs and BiO2-x. As shown in Fig. 9 a, green represents the electron-rich region and yellow represents the electron-depleted region. The results reveal that the electrons could effectively transfer at the interface of N-GQDs and the (110) surface of BiO2-x. In order to further reveal the contributions of the N-GQDs to the electronic structure of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite, the DOS of N-GQDs/BiO2-x are calculated (Fig. 9b). Modified by the ultrasmall N-GQDs with good conductivity, the band gap of N-GQDs/BiO2-x is almost invisible, suggesting that the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite exhibited a lower charge transport resistance and enhanced light harvesting. Besides, the blue, green and red lines in Fig. 9b represent the total density of states (TDOS) of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite, the partial density of states (PDOS) of BiO2-x and N-GQDs in the N-GQDs/BiO2-x composite, respectively. Because BiO2-x was the main component of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x, the PDOS of BiO2-x was thus similar to the TDOS of the N-GQDs/BiO2-x. The calculated electronic structures of N-GQDs and BiO2-x show reasonable matching for the formation of 0D/2D heterojunction. Moreover, the work functions of BiO2-x and N-GQDs/BiO2-x were 4.93 eV (Fig. 9c) and 4.60 eV (Fig. 9d), respectively. The work function decreased after N-GQDs were attached to the BiO2-x surface, which facilitated the escape of electrons from the catalyst surface and promoted the transfer of the photogenerated electrons. As a consequence, more
O2 − and
OH radicals would be produced through the molecular oxygen activation process in photocatalysis.

With the above results, a possible mechanism for the photocatalytic reaction over the GBO-0.4 composite is proposed (Scheme 1 ). The enhanced photocatalytic performance is attributed to the following aspects: (I) the broader light absorption and more photoinduced production of electrons and holes; (II) the accelerated photoexcited electron/hole pairs separation and migration ability. The upconversion effect caused by the N-GQDs could widen the light absorption from 852 nm to 895 nm and the longer-wavelength light is converted to a shorter wavelength, which results in more photoinduced charges. The N-GQDs also serve as an electron sink and reservoir to favor rapid separation and transfer efficiency of photogenerated electron/hole pairs.

In photocatalysis, electrons are excited and transferred from VB to CB of BiO2-x. Simultaneously, the photogenerated electrons are transformed onto the Ov site. The Ov might be more important under NIR light due to the resultant lower bandgap energy with longer light absorption and the effect of electrons capturing center. Because of the CB potential (−0.50 eV vs. NHE) of BiO2-x was more negative than the standard reduction potential of O2/
O2 − (−0.33 eV vs. NHE), the accumulated electrons on the CB and Ov could capture the dissolved oxygen in aqueous solution to produce
O2 – via one-electron reduction process (Eq. (3)).
O2 – could be partially transformed into
OH through the reactions with H+ via a two-electron reduction process (Eqs. (4) and (5)). In this way, the activation of molecular oxygen under full solar light is realized and the formation of
O2 − and
OH is substantially accelerated. The photo-induced holes remained in the VB position of BiO2-x could not oxidize the OH− or water molecules to produce
OH radical because of the VB potential (+0.96 eV vs. NHE) is less positive than OH−/
OH potential (+2.40 eV vs. NHE) and H2O/
OH potential (+2.72 eV vs. NHE). The holes could oxidize TC directly and its contribution is enormous under full-spectrum, visible, especially NIR light irradiations. The redox ability of the photogenerated electrons and holes is synchronously reinforced. Therefore, the main reactive species including h+,
O2 − and
OH are responsible for the TC degradation, synergistically facilitating the full-spectrum-responsive decontamination over the N-GQDs/BiO2-x hybrid materials.
(3)
e -
+
O 2
→ •
O 2 -
(4)
•
O 2 -
+
e -
+ 2
H +
→
H 2
O 2
(5)
H 2
O 2
+
e -
→
OH -
+ • OH

ce:section.ce:section-title: TC degradation pathway by the GBO-0.4 composite under full-spectrum light
ce:section.ce:para:
The intermediates formed in the photocatalytic degradation of TC over the GBO-0.4 were identified by LC–MS. The detailed main products are listed in Table S1. The potential TC degradation pathways including hydroxylation, dealkylation, and dehydration are illustrated in Scheme 2 . The TC molecule with a m/z value at 445 was first attacked by the
OH radical to form the hydroxylated product P2 (m/z = 461) and then reacted with photo-induced holes (h+) via the dealkylation process to boost the secondary product P5 (m/z=433) [40]. TC molecule could also be transformed into the products P3 (m/z= 427) and P4 (m/z=384), which were suggested as the dehydration products of TC. The generated
O2 − might attack the compound P3 via the dealkylation process to produce intermediate P6 (m/z=396). As the reactions continued, ring-opening was triggered to accelerate the TC mineralization. The products P4 (m/z =384), P5 (m/z=433) and P6 (m/z=396) were then converted into the products P7 (m/z =369) and P8 (m/z=376), respectively. With the prolonged reaction time, the above-mentioned products were further oxidized to the products P9 (m/z=279) and P10 (m/z=121) through the side chains breaking and ring-opening [30,41]. Finally, the generated intermediates would be mineralized into CO2 and H2O and other smaller-molecular inorganic molecular products.

The N-GQDs modified BiO2-x nanosheets prepared in this work exhibit a substantially enhanced photocatalytic activity for the TC degradation than the pure BiO2-x. The GBO-0.4 shows 4.0-, 2.9- and 5.5-fold higher TC degradation rate than the BiO2-x under full-spectrum, visible and even NIR light irradiations, respectively. The synergistic effects between the N-GQDs and BiO2-x include the broader light absorption, enhanced photogenerated charge separation, and more reactive oxygen species generation through efficient molecular oxygen activation. Abundant
O2 − is produced to favor rapid electron transfer and participates in the secondary reactions for
OH generation. The main TC degradation products are identified and insightful mechanism of the full-spectrum photocatalytic mechanism over GBO-0.4 is proposed. The design principles outlined in this work could be extended to the synthesis of other nanoscale hybrid materials for highly efficient and broad-spectral photocatalytic applications in pollutant degradation.

Fei Chen conceived and planned the experiments and carried out relative experiments. Lian-Lian Liu, Jie-Jie Chen, and Ying-Jie Zhang contributed to the DFT calculations. Fei Chen, Gui-Xiang Huang, Jing-Hang Wu, and Qi Yang conducted and analyzed the various characterizations. Fei Chen wrote the initial draft of the manuscript, and further modified by Jie-Jie Chen and Han-Qing Yu. All authors have given approval to the final version of the manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the National Key R&D Program of China (2018YFC0406303), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21590812, 51538011, 51908528 and 51821006), the Postdoctoral Innovation Talent Support Program of China (BX20180290), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (Grant No. 2018M640595) and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (WK2060120001) for supporting this work. The numerical calculations in this work have been conducted on the supercomputing system in the Supercomputing Center of University of Science and Technology of China.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary material related to this article can be found, in the online version, at doi:https://doi.org/10.1016/j.apcatb.2020.119218.

ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:
